description: Evaluates whether the FirebaseUI Web integration-helper skill improves correctness versus a no-skill baseline.

evals.name: React Vite email-password integration
evals.prompt: Add FirebaseUI Web v7 to this Vite React app for email/password sign-in. Keep Firebase initialization in a reusable client-safe module, render a sign-in screen from the React package, include the FirebaseUI styles, and use placeholder Firebase config from environment variables. Update package.json dependencies as needed.
evals.expected_output: A Vite React app wired to FirebaseUI Web v7 with Firebase initialization, a FirebaseUI provider, an email/password sign-in screen, styles imported, and public package dependencies.

deterministic_assertions.description: Uses FirebaseUIProvider from the React package
Renders a FirebaseUI email/password capable sign-in screen
Does not import internal repository aliases

llm_assertions: The implementation keeps Firebase app/UI initialization separate from the rendered React screen.
The answer tells the developer that Email/Password must be enabled in Firebase Console.
The implementation uses FirebaseUI Web v7 public package imports rather than legacy firebaseui-web v6 APIs.

evals.name: Next.js App Router Google OAuth integration
evals.prompt: Integrate FirebaseUI Web v7 into this Next.js App Router app for Google sign-in. Respect App Router client/server boundaries, keep FirebaseUI rendering in client components, use environment-backed Firebase config, add the required packages and styles, and leave server-side auth checks out of scope unless they are clearly separated.
evals.expected_output: A Next.js App Router app with client-only FirebaseUI initialization/rendering, Google sign-in UI, styles, public dependencies, and no server component FirebaseUI misuse.

deterministic_assertions.description: Marks FirebaseUI-rendering code as a client component
Does not import internal repository aliases

llm_assertions: FirebaseUI components and initializeUI usage are isolated to client components or client-only modules.
The implementation does not imply that rendering FirebaseUI on the server solves SSR authentication.
The answer tells the developer to enable Google sign-in in Firebase Console.

evals.name: Angular provideFirebaseUI setup
evals.prompt: Add FirebaseUI Web v7 to this Angular app for email/password sign-in. Use Angular's application config/provider style, add provideFirebaseUI, initialize FirebaseUI once, render a FirebaseUI auth component, include styles, and update dependencies.
evals.expected_output: An Angular app configured with Firebase and provideFirebaseUI, rendering FirebaseUI auth UI through the Angular package with styles and dependencies.

deterministic_assertions.description: Renders a FirebaseUI Angular component or screen

llm_assertions: The Angular setup uses provideFirebaseUI rather than React provider patterns.
The FirebaseUI store is initialized once and supplied through Angular provider configuration.
The answer tells the developer to enable Email/Password in Firebase Console.

evals.name: shadcn/headless React integration
evals.prompt: Wire this shadcn-style Vite React app to FirebaseUI Web v7 using the headless/core APIs plus React provider. Keep the existing local Button component, add Firebase initialization, wrap with FirebaseUIProvider, and build a small sign-in panel that can use FirebaseUI auth state/actions without importing internal repo aliases.
evals.expected_output: A shadcn-style React app that keeps local UI components, uses FirebaseUI core/React public packages, initializes FirebaseUI, and avoids internal aliases.

deterministic_assertions.description: Keeps the local shadcn-style Button component
Does not import internal repository aliases

llm_assertions: The implementation preserves the app's shadcn-style local components instead of replacing the app with a generic demo.
The integration uses public core/React FirebaseUI package imports.
The implementation explains which Firebase Console providers must be enabled for the chosen flow.

evals.name: Custom OAuth 2.0 token exchange
evals.prompt: This app needs to sign users in with a custom OAuth 2.0 provider that is not OIDC, like Snapchat Login Kit. Add a minimal client/server integration plan in code: the client starts the provider flow and signs in to Firebase with a custom token returned from the backend; the backend exchanges an authorization code, resolves a stable provider user id, mints a Firebase custom token, and never exposes the provider client secret to the browser.
evals.expected_output: A small client/server implementation sketch using Firebase custom-token sign-in for a non-OIDC OAuth provider, with server-side token exchange and clear secret handling.

deterministic_assertions.description: Client signs in with a Firebase custom token
Backend mints or clearly stubs Firebase custom token creation
Does not place provider client secret in browser-facing Vite env

llm_assertions: The implementation clearly explains that non-OIDC OAuth providers cannot be configured directly as Firebase OIDC providers.
Provider client secrets and token exchange stay on the backend.
The Firebase UID is based on a stable provider user identifier.
